title: The Comprehensive Clinician_s Guide to_ Cognitive Behavioral
book: The Comprehensive Clinician_s Guide to_ Cognitive Behavioral

node_types.description: A person receiving mental health treatment; stores demographic, clinical, and psychosocial information relevant to therapy.

properties.name: history_of_present_illness
properties.description: Narrative of current symptoms and their development

properties.name: personal_history
properties.description: Relevant personal, social, and medical history

properties.name: mental_status
properties.description: Summary of client's current mental state

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A formal clinical diagnosis assigned to a client, including DSM-5/ICD-10 codes and disorder details.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A psychological, emotional, behavioral, or physiological symptom experienced by the client.

properties.name: symptom_type
properties.description: Type of symptom (e.g., emotional, cognitive, behavioral, physiological)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A deeply held, often negative, belief about the self, others, or the world (e.g., 'I am unlovable').

properties.name: domain
properties.description: Domain of belief (e.g., self, world, future, social, achievement)

properties.name: origin
properties.description: Origin or shaping experience of the belief

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: An immediate, often unconscious, thought triggered by an event or situation.

properties.name: trigger
properties.description: Event or situation that triggered the thought

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: An emotional state experienced by the client in response to thoughts or situations.

properties.name: emotion_type
properties.description: Type of emotion (e.g., sadness, anxiety, anger)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A specific action or behavioral pattern exhibited by the client, including adaptive and maladaptive behaviors.

properties.name: behavior_type
properties.description: Type of behavior (e.g., avoidance, social withdrawal, self-harm)

properties.name: context
properties.description: Situation or context in which the behavior occurs

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A behavioral or cognitive strategy used to cope with or compensate for negative beliefs or emotions.

properties.name: strategy_type
properties.description: Type of strategy (e.g., avoidance, perfectionism, safety behavior)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: An internal or external event or stimulus that initiates a cognitive, emotional, or behavioral response.

properties.name: trigger_type
properties.description: Type of trigger (e.g., situation, thought, physiological sensation)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A specific, measurable objective collaboratively set for therapy.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A specific therapeutic technique or strategy applied in treatment.

properties.name: intervention_type
properties.description: Type of intervention (e.g., cognitive restructuring, exposure, behavioral activation)

properties.name: session_number
properties.description: Session in which intervention was applied

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A structured tool or template used to guide client reflection, assessment, or intervention.

properties.name: worksheet_type
properties.description: Type of worksheet (e.g., thought record, activity schedule, anger log)

properties.name: description
properties.description: Purpose and content of the worksheet

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

node_types.description: A cultural, religious, or value-based factor relevant to the client's conceptualization or treatment.

properties.name: factor_type
properties.description: Type of factor (e.g., religious belief, cultural norm, value)

properties.name: relevance
properties.description: How the factor influences conceptualization or treatment

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client

node_types.description: A mental health professional providing treatment to the client.

properties.name: cultural_alignment
properties.description: Therapist's cultural or religious alignment, if relevant

node_types.description: A single therapy session, including agenda, interventions, and outcomes.

properties.name: agenda
properties.description: Planned topics or goals for the session

properties.name: summary
properties.description: Summary of session content and outcomes

properties.name: homework_assigned
properties.description: Homework or tasks assigned to the client

edge_types.description: Indicates that a client has been assigned a specific diagnosis.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a client is experiencing a particular symptom.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a client holds a specific core belief.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a client experiences a specific automatic thought.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that an automatic thought leads to a specific emotional response.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that an emotional state leads to a specific behavior.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a behavior serves as a compensatory strategy for coping with distress or negative beliefs.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a trigger initiates a specific automatic thought.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a core belief shapes or biases the content of an automatic thought.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a client has a specific treatment goal.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a specific intervention is used to address a treatment goal.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that an intervention involves the use of a specific worksheet or tool.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client
Chapter 9: Personality Disorders, Self-Harm, and Suicidality

edge_types.description: Indicates that a client's conceptualization or treatment is influenced by a specific cultural or religious factor.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2: Cognitive Conceptualization: Understanding the Client

edge_types.description: Indicates that a therapist conducts a specific therapy session.
Indicates that a specific intervention was delivered during a session.
Indicates that a worksheet or homework was assigned during a session.
